diagrams.title: Image Digitization Process
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing how continuous images are converted to digital form through sampling and quantization
elements: Input continuous image with infinite detail
Sampling block: discretizes spatial coordinates (x,y) into rows and columns
Output of sampling: discrete spatial grid (pixels at specific locations)
Quantization block: converts continuous intensity values to discrete levels (0-255 for 8-bit)
Final digital image output: discrete 2D array of quantized values
Arrows showing data flow from continuous to discrete representation
Labels indicating: sampling rate determines spatial resolution, quantization levels determine intensity resolution
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the two-step process of converting analog images to digital format, and how sampling affects spatial resolution while quantization affects intensity levels

diagrams.title: Digital Image as 2D Array
diagrams.description: Visual representation of a grayscale image as a matrix of pixel values with coordinate system
elements: Grid of squares representing pixels arranged in M rows x N columns
Coordinate axes showing x (column) and y (row) directions
Sample pixel values (0-255) displayed inside each cell
Color gradient from black (0) to white (255) across the grid
Pixel at position (x,y) highlighted with marker
Annotation showing image function f(x,y) = intensity value
Zoomed inset showing single pixel with its location and value
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize how a digital image is stored in computer memory as a 2D array, understand pixel coordinates, and connect the mathematical representation f(x,y) to actual pixel values

diagrams.title: Color Space Relationships
diagrams.description: A diagram showing different color spaces and their relationships with common conversion paths
elements: Central cube/cylinder representing the color spectrum
RGB color space as 3D cube with R, G, B axes (corners: black, red, green, blue, cyan, magenta, yellow, white)
HSV color space as cone/cylinder with Hue, Saturation, Value axes
YCbCr color space showing Y (luminance), Cb (blue-difference), Cr (red-difference) components
LAB color space showing L (lightness), a (green-red), b (blue-yellow) axes
Arrows connecting spaces with labels showing conversion formulas
Practical application icons near each space: RGB (displays), HSV (image processing), YCbCr (video compression), LAB (color correction)
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the structure of different color spaces, their advantages for specific applications, and how to convert between them for various computer vision tasks

diagrams.title: Pixel Neighborhoods and Connectivity
diagrams.description: Diagram showing 4-neighborhood and 8-neighborhood connectivity around a center pixel
elements: Central pixel at coordinate (x,y) highlighted in distinct color
4-neighborhood: pixels at (x-1,y), (x+1,y), (x,y-1), (x,y+1) shown with connections
8-neighborhood: all 8 surrounding pixels shown with diagonal connections
Diagonal neighborhood: pixels at (x-1,y-1), (x-1,y+1), (x+1,y-1), (x+1,y+1)
Boundary conditions illustrated for edge and corner pixels
Color coding: different colors for each neighborhood type
Legend explaining when each neighborhood type is used
diagrams.purpose: Students understand pixel adjacency concepts essential for image segmentation, morphological operations, and region growing algorithms

diagrams.title: Image Resolution Types
diagrams.description: Comparison diagram showing impact of different spatial and intensity resolutions on image quality
elements: Four sample images showing same scene at different resolutions
Low spatial resolution (64x64) vs High spatial resolution (512x512)
Low intensity resolution (2-bit = 4 levels) vs High intensity resolution (8-bit = 256 levels)
Visual examples of: posterization effect (fewer intensity levels), pixelation effect (lower spatial resolution)
Graph or bar chart showing file size relationship with resolution
Axis labels: Spatial Resolution (pixels) vs File Size
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the difference between spatial and intensity resolution, their impact on image quality, and the trade-off with storage requirements

charts.title: Analog vs Digital Image Characteristics
charts.content: Table with columns: Aspect, Analog/Continuous, Digital/Discrete. Rows: Spatial Domain (continuous/infinite vs discrete/finite pixels), Intensity Values (continuous/infinite vs discrete/finite levels), Storage Medium (film, photographic paper vs computer memory), Processing Method (chemical/optical vs computational/digital), Reproducibility (degradation with copying vs identical copies), Editability (limited vs highly flexible)

charts.title: Color Spaces Comparison
charts.content: Table with columns: Color Space, Components, Range, Primary Use Cases, Advantages. Rows: RGB (R,G,B; 0-255; displays, cameras; intuitive for hardware), HSV (Hue 0-360, Saturation 0-100, Value 0-100; image processing, segmentation; matches human perception), YCbCr (Y 0-255, Cb/Cr 0-255; video compression, JPEG; separates luminance from chrominance), LAB (L 0-100, a -128 to 127, b -128 to 127; color correction, printing; device-independent)

charts.title: Image Types Classification
charts.content: Hierarchical chart showing: Image Types > Binary (1-bit, black/white only, documents, barcodes) > Grayscale (8-bit, 256 levels, medical imaging, night vision) > Color (24-bit RGB, 16.7M colors, photography, video) > Multi-spectral (multiple bands, remote sensing, satellite imagery). Include practical examples for each type.

charts.title: Image Digitization Decision Process
charts.content: Flowchart: Start > Is image already digital? (Yes → Use directly, No → Continue) > Apply Sampling (choose resolution: low/medium/high) > Apply Quantization (choose bit-depth: 1-bit/8-bit/24-bit) > Store as Digital Format (PNG, JPEG, BMP) > End. Include decision points with arrows showing different paths.

branches: 2. Digitization Process: Sampling (spatial discretization) + Quantization (intensity discretization)
3. Pixel Properties: Location (x,y), Value (intensity/color), Addressable unit
4. Image Types: Binary (1-bit), Grayscale (8-bit), Color (RGB, HSV, YCbCr, LAB)
5. Resolution: Spatial (pixel count), Intensity (bit depth), Temporal (frame rate)
7. Operations: Pixel neighborhoods (4-connectivity, 8-connectivity), Distance metrics (Euclidean, City-block, Chessboard)
8. Storage: File size calculation (rows × columns × bytes per pixel)
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all fundamental concepts from image definition through storage, enabling students to see relationships between theoretical concepts and practical applications in computer vision
